title: Bank of England Changes Money Pictures to Animals
summary: The Bank of England is planning to put pictures of British wildlife, like beavers, on its new banknotes instead of famous people such as Sir Winston Churchill and Jane Austen. This decision has made some politicians upset because they think it is silly and erases history. The Bank says it is doing this to make the money harder to copy and because the public wants it. Groups like the RSPCA are suggesting animals like pigeons and rats for the notes. A poll shows that many people support having nature on the notes, but others prefer historical figures. The Bank has not yet chosen which animals will appear.

animals and plants that live in nature
people who lead and make decisions in government
asking people what they think before making a choice

options: To make money harder to copy
questions.correct_answer: To make money harder to copy

What did a poll show about people's opinions?
Have the specific animals for the notes been chosen yet?

background_read: Banknotes are paper money used to buy things. In the UK, the Bank of England makes banknotes with pictures of important people like Winston Churchill, who was a prime minister during World War II, and Jane Austen, a famous writer. Now, the Bank wants to change these pictures to animals from British nature, such as beavers or birds. This is because they want to make the notes safer from counterfeiters and because many people asked for it in a survey. Some leaders disagree, saying it removes history, but others think it is a good idea to celebrate wildlife.
Article_Structure: WHO: The Bank of England, politicians like Kemi Badenoch and Nigel Farage, and groups like the RSPCA.
WHAT: Changing pictures on banknotes from historical figures to British wildlife.
WHY: To prevent counterfeiting and reflect public wishes, though some oppose it as erasing history.

perspectives.description: People and the Bank believe it makes notes safer and celebrates nature.
Politicians think it is silly and removes important historical figures from money.
